1: 'In the seventh month, on the twenty-first day of the month, the word of YHWH came by the hand of Haggai the prophet.' The date is 17 October 520 BCE — the last day of the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot), which ran from month 7 day 15 to day 21. This was one of the three great pilgrimage festivals, a time when the entire community was assembled. The choice of Sukkot's closing day for this oracle is significant: Sukkot celebrated both the wilderness wandering (temporary shelters = God's provision during exodus) and the harvest (thanksgiving for the land's produce). A festival about God's presence in a temporary dwelling is the perfect occasion for an oracle about the permanent dwelling being rebuilt.
2: 'Speak now to Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and to all the remnant of the people, and say.' The same three-part address as Haggai 1:1 — Zerubbabel, Joshua, and all the remnant. The civil and religious leadership together with the whole community. This consistent address pattern emphasizes that the restoration project is a community-wide endeavor requiring unified leadership and popular participation.
3: 'Who is left among you who saw this house in its former glory? How do you see it now? Is it not as nothing in your eyes?' The devastating question: those who remembered Solomon's temple (destroyed 586 BCE) and are now watching the modest second temple take shape must make the comparison. Some of the older returnees would indeed have seen the first temple in their youth — Ezra 3:12 records the older men weeping when the foundation of the second temple was laid, while the younger generation shouted for joy. The contrast in scale and beauty was visible and painful. Haggai acknowledges this honestly rather than dismissing it.
4: 'Yet now be strong, O Zerubbabel, declares YHWH. Be strong, O Joshua, son of Jehozadak, the high priest. Be strong, all you people of the land, declares YHWH. Work, for I am with you, declares YHWH of hosts.' The threefold encouragement mirrors the Deuteronomic commissioning formula for Joshua: 'be strong and courageous' (chazaq ve'ematz — Joshua 1:6, 7, 9, 18). The same formula that inaugurated the conquest of the land now inaugurates the restoration of the temple. Ani itchem (I am with you) is again the divine-presence guarantee — first given in Haggai 1:13, now reiterated as the foundation for work.
5: 'According to the covenant that I made with you when you came out of Egypt. My Spirit remains in your midst. Fear not.' The rebuilding project is grounded in the Sinai covenant and the Exodus — the definitive acts of covenant formation. YHWH's Spirit (ruachi omedet betochechem, 'my Spirit stands/remains in your midst') is the abiding divine presence that accompanied Israel through the wilderness in the cloud and fire (Exodus 13:21-22; Haggai 14:19-20; Numbers 11:25). The same Spirit that hovered over the wilderness tabernacle hovers over the post-exilic building site. Fear not: the fear of the community in the face of Persian opposition (Ezra 4) or the disappointing scale of the new temple is answered with the covenant-presence promise.
6: 'For thus says YHWH of hosts: Yet once more, in a little while, I will shake the heavens and the earth and the sea and the dry land.' The eschatological shaking oracle: the cosmic disturbance that will accompany YHWH's ultimate intervention. 'Yet once more' (od achat me'at hi) — one more decisive shaking event, in a brief interval. The heavens, earth, sea, and dry land are the four categories of creation established in Genesis 1. A shaking of all four means a total disruption of the created order. This verse is quoted in Hebrews 12:26-27 to describe the eschatological removal of the created order in favor of the unshakeable kingdom.
7: 'And I will shake all nations, so that the treasures of all nations shall come in, and I will fill this house with glory, says YHWH of hosts.' The shaking of the nations produces the flow of nations' treasures (chemdat kol hagoyim) to the temple. The Hebrew chemdat (singular construct of chemdah, desire/treasure) is the LXX's basis for its plural translation; the Vulgate read it as a singular noun pointing to the Messiah ('the Desired of All Nations'), giving rise to a long Christological interpretation. Most modern scholars prefer the collective reading: the wealth/treasures of all nations flowing to Zion. Either way, the restored temple will be filled with a glory surpassing the first.
8: 'The silver is mine, and the gold is mine, declares YHWH of hosts.' The resources for temple building are YHWH's own — he is not constrained by the community's poverty. This verse answers the practical objection that the post-exilic community cannot fund a temple comparable to Solomon's (who received tribute from the entire ancient Near East). YHWH owns all silver and gold; the human challenge is not a supply problem but an obedience problem.
9: 'The latter glory of this house shall be greater than the former, says YHWH of hosts. And in this place I will give peace, declares YHWH of hosts.' The promise of surpassing glory: the second temple's ultimate glory will exceed Solomon's. This promise was controversial in its immediate fulfillment — the second temple as built by Zerubbabel was plainly less magnificent than Solomon's. The fulfillment was seen in Herod's elaborately rebuilt temple (which was technically still the 'second temple') or, in Christian reading, in the presence of Jesus (John 2:19-21: 'destroy this temple and in three days I will raise it up'). Shalom (peace) — the covenant blessing of YHWH's dwelling in the midst of his people, as in Numbers 6:24-26.
10: 'On the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month, in the second year of Darius, the word of YHWH came by Haggai the prophet.' The third oracle date: 18 December 520 BCE — three months after work began. The ninth month is the rainy season in Israel, when the agricultural calendar's anxieties about harvest turn to planting hopes. The specific date three months into the project allows Haggai to make a concrete historical observation about the agricultural situation before and after the rebuilding began.
11: 'Thus says YHWH of hosts: Ask the priests about the law.' Haggai engages the priests in a halakhic question — a pedagogical method of using a legal case to establish a theological principle. The priests were the authoritative interpreters of Torah (Deuteronomy 17:8-13; Malachi 2:7); asking them to rule on a specific case before making the theological application is methodologically precise. The double question that follows is about ritual contagion — the transmission of holiness and impurity — as a framework for the community's spiritual situation.
12: 'If someone carries holy meat in the fold of his garment and touches with his fold bread or stew or wine or oil or any kind of food, does it become holy?' The priests said, No.' The first halakhic case: does holiness transfer by secondary contact? The answer is no — holiness cannot be passed on through a secondary medium (garment carrying holy meat touching another food). This is the standard halakha as understood in Second Temple Judaism: holiness is not contagious in the way impurity is.
13: 'Then Haggai said, If someone who is unclean by contact with a dead body touches any of these, does it become unclean? The priests answered, It does become unclean.' The second case: does impurity transfer through secondary contact? Yes — corpse impurity (tum'at met, the most severe category of ritual impurity in Leviticus 21:1-4; Numbers 19) does transfer through secondary contact. The asymmetry is the key: holiness does not spread outward, but impurity does. Holiness requires proximity to the source; impurity propagates through contact.
14: 'Then Haggai answered and said, So is it with this people, and with this nation before me, declares YHWH, and so with every work of their hands. And what they offer there is unclean.' The application of the halakhic asymmetry: the people's uncleanness (moral/spiritual — their neglect of the covenant, their deferred obedience) has contaminated all their work and all their offerings. The temple that is being rebuilt can transmit holiness only when the community itself is in a state of covenant faithfulness. Unclean hands produce unclean worship, even if the form is technically correct.
15: 'Now then, consider from this day onward. Before stone was placed upon stone in the temple of YHWH, how did you fare?' The fourth call to consideration (cf. Haggai 1:5, 7). Haggai asks the people to perform the diagnostic comparison: what was life like before the rebuilding began (before this day, 18 December 520 BCE = three months after work began, but Haggai may be looking back to before day 24 of month 6 = 21 September)? The agricultural record before and after the commitment to build will be the evidence.
16: 'When one came to a heap of twenty measures, there were but ten. When one came to the wine vat to draw fifty measures, there were but twenty.' The quantified experience of covenant-curse-level scarcity: harvests consistently produced half of what they should. Twenty-measure grain heaps yielded ten; fifty-measure wine-vat draws yielded twenty. The shortfalls are not catastrophic but steady — a pattern of consistent underperformance that should have prompted reflection. Haggai's genius is not to announce new punishment but to reframe what the people had already experienced as evidence of their covenant situation.
17: 'I struck you and all the products of your toil with blight and with mildew and with hail, yet you did not turn to me, declares YHWH.' The agricultural interventions: blight (shidafon), mildew (yerayon), and hail (barad) — the three standard Deuteronomic curses (Deuteronomy 28:22; Amos 4:9 uses the same pair). The phrase 'yet you did not turn to me' (ve'en etchem elai) is identical to Amos 4:6-11's refrain — YHWH's repeated judgments that produced no repentance. Haggai situates the post-exilic community in the same failure-to-respond pattern as the pre-exilic generation.
18: 'Consider from this day onward, from the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month. Since the day that the foundation of YHWH's temple was laid, consider.' The fifth and final call to consideration — now anchoring to the specific date: the twenty-fourth of the ninth month (18 December 520 BCE), when the oracle is delivered. This date marks the transition point: before this day, the covenant-curse pattern; from this day forward, the covenant-blessing pattern. Haggai is asking the people to observe what happens to the harvest from this turning point onward.
19: 'Is the seed yet in the barn? Indeed, the vine, the fig tree, the pomegranate, and the olive tree have yielded nothing. But from this day on I will bless you.' In December, the seed is in the ground — not yet visible, not yet rewarding the farmer's hope. The fruit trees are bare. But from this day — the day of full commitment to rebuilding, the day of this oracle — YHWH declares the blessing: me'hayom hazeh avareich, 'from this day I will bless.' The blessing is prospective and immediate. The agricultural blessing that had been withheld will now flow — not because the temple is complete, but because the community has turned its heart to YHWH's house.
20: 'The word of YHWH came a second time to Haggai on the twenty-fourth day of the month.' The fourth oracle is delivered on the same day as the third (18 December 520 BCE), but addressed exclusively to Zerubbabel. Two oracles on the same day — the first (Haggai 2:10-19) addressed to the community, the second (Haggai 2:20-23) addressed to the Davidic governor personally. The double oracle on a single day has no other parallel in the prophetic corpus.
21: 'Speak to Zerubbabel, governor of Judah, saying, I am about to shake the heavens and the earth.' The cosmic shaking oracle of Haggai 2:6 is now applied personally to Zerubbabel. The connection between the universal shaking and the specific Davidic figure establishes that the shaking of the nations is the context within which YHWH's special favor toward Zerubbabel is exercised. The shaking of creation is not merely cataclysm but the preparation for messianic establishment.
22: 'And I am about to overthrow the throne of kingdoms. I will destroy the strength of the kingdoms of the nations, and overthrow the chariots and their riders. And the horses and their riders shall go down, every one by the sword of his brother.' The military collapse of the nations: thrones overturned, kingdoms destroyed, chariots and horsemen brought down by fratricidal warfare. The 'sword of his brother' recalls the Gideon narrative (Judges 7:22) and the Jehoshaphat narrative (2 Chronicles 20:23) where YHWH set the enemy nations against each other. The Persian empire will not endure; the kingdoms that oppose YHWH's purposes will destroy each other.
23: 'On that day, declares YHWH of hosts, I will take you, O Zerubbabel my servant, the son of Shealtiel, declares YHWH, and make you like a signet ring, for I have chosen you, declares YHWH of hosts.' The signet ring (chatam) oracle — the reversal of the curse on Jehoiachin. Jeremiah 22:24 had declared that YHWH would tear Jehoiachin (Coniah) from his right hand like a signet ring — the Davidic dynasty was, in effect, de-signetted, its authority revoked. Now YHWH declares Zerubbabel — Jehoiachin's grandson — to be his signet ring: the Davidic authority is re-established, the curse reversed. The Davidic covenant is renewed in Zerubbabel, pointing beyond him to the ultimate Davidic ruler (Matthew 1:12 includes both Jehoiachin and Zerubbabel in Jesus's genealogy). Three divine declarations punctuate the verse: 'declares YHWH of hosts,' 'declares YHWH,' 'declares YHWH of hosts' — the triple witness amplifying the irrevocable character of the promise.
